course_description:
This course will introduce students to the central legal concepts and principles in occupational health and safety, including the history and development of OH&S legislation, the relationship between OH&S law and industrial law, and the relationship between OH&S legislation and common law.

LAWS7024 ﾠwill introduce students to the central legal concepts and principles in occupational health and safety, including the Workplace Health and Safety Act, Regulations and Codes of Practice. Studentsﾠwill also apply these legal concepts and principles in a Moot Court Exercise.

course_aims:
Course Aims

This course aims to explore the concepts and principles underlying current Australian Workplace Health and Safety legislation. An understanding of these principles underpins the provision of OHS advice and OHS decision making in organisations.

learning_outcomes.description: Demonstrate a comprehensive understanding of Workplace Health and Safety laws that impact on the management of OHS.
Demonstrate an understanding of how other Laws impact on the management of OHS.
Identify and apply relevant Laws to the management of OHS.
Demonstrate an understanding of legal principles and processes in the context of OHS.
Ability to communicate legal requirements to relevant stakeholders and to identify when to seek professional legal advice.

assessment_details.title: Workshop One Participation
assessment_details.task_description: Students must attend both days of course delivery and complete workshop activities collaboratively (groups of 3 or 4 students) and report back on completed activities as required. Active participation is weighted at 5% each day. Participation marks will be pro-rated, to obtain the full 5% you will need to be in the class for the entire day (9:00am – 4:00pm), any absences greater than 30mins will result in a deduction in line with the following: Absent 30mins - 1 hour: maximum of 4 marks Absent 1-2 hours: maximum of 3 marks Absent 2-4 hours: maximum of 2 marks Absent 4+ hours: maximum of 1 marks If students are absent for medical reasons they will need to liaise directly with the course coordinator to arrange alternative assessment Read full task description Hide full task description

assessment_details.title: Case Study Assignment
assessment_details.task_description: Students will be presented with a Case Study based on the in-class activities from the first weekend. Students will be required to answer 4 questions based on the Case Study provided. Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Case study is to be submitted via Turnitin.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
See the Additional assessment information section below for information relating to extension applications.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
You are required to submit assessable items on time. If you fail to meet the submission deadline for any assessment item, then 10% of the maximum possible mark for the assessment item (the assessment ‘marked from’ value) will be deducted as a late penalty for every day (or part day) late after the due date.
For example, if you submit your assignment 1 hour late, you will be penalised 10%; if your assignment is 24.5 hours late, you will be penalised 20% (because it is late by one 24-hour period plus part of another 24-hour period).

assessment_details.title: Moot Court
assessment_details.category: Paper/ Report/ Annotation, Reflection, Role play/ Simulation
assessment_details.due_date: Part 1: Plan 31/08/2025 9:00 am Part 2: Role Play 31/08/2025 9:00 am Part 3: Review/Reflection 12/09/2025 9:00 am The role play will occur during the teaching session between 9am and 11am.
assessment_details.other_conditions: Student specific. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: The Moot Court exercise involves a detailed hypothetical OHS legal case. Students will be allocated specific tasks relating to the hypothetical OHS legal case. Students are required to: Part 1: Plan: students will research their allocated tasks and develop a written plan. [10%] Part 2: Role Play: students will participate in a role play and deliver an oral presentation based on their allocated task/s for the Moot Court Exercise. [30%] HURDLE Part 3: Case Review: Students will provide a written case review/reflection for the Moot Court Exercise. [20%] Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools. Read full task description Hide full task description

Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
Late submission penalties only apply to Part 3 [Review/reflection]. If extenuating circumstances result in the student not attending on Sunday 31st October and thereby missing the role play session students will need to liaise directly with the course coordinator to arrange alternative assessment.

learning_activities.topic: OHS Law Workshops on the first weekend will cover: Introduction to OHS law in Australia; OHS duties; Consultation and entry; Incident Notification; Risk Management; Codes of Practice; Workers' compensation; Concept of Duty - Negligence; Inspectors and enforcement; Offences, Self Incrimination and Defences; Fair Work Act and Protections

Preparation for Moot Court Exercise and Moot Court These workshops will firstly prepare students for the Moot Court exercise which involves a detailed hypothetical OHS legal case. Students will be provided with the facts of the case, relevant materials and important concepts will be discussed. Students will be allocated a task and required to research the issues related to their allocated task. This preparation will be applied to the Moot Court which involves student oral presentations (role plays) in a simulated court and a debrief of the exercise.
